title: Cackle Fever
summary: A disease that causes fits of uncontrollable laughter, eventually incapacitating the victim.
content:
## Cackle Fever

**Contraction:** Exposure to an infected humanoid (airborne, requires proximity)
**Incubation:** 1d4 hours

Symptoms manifest 1d4 hours after infection and include fever and disorientation. The infected creature gains one level of exhaustion that can't be removed until the disease is cured.

### Effects
Any event that causes the infected creature great stress—including entering combat, taking damage, experiencing fear, or having a nightmare—forces the creature to make a DC 13 Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, the creature takes 5 (1d10) psychic damage and becomes incapacitated with mad laughter for 1 minute. The creature can repeat the saving throw at the end of each of its turns, ending the mad laughter and the incapacitated condition on a success.

### Contagion
Any humanoid creature that starts its turn within 10 feet of an infected creature in the throes of mad laughter must succeed on a DC 10 Constitution saving throw or also become infected with the disease. Once a creature succeeds on this save, it is immune to the mad laughter of that particular infected creature for 24 hours.

### Cure
At the end of each long rest, an infected creature can make a DC 13 Constitution saving throw. On a successful save, the DC for this save and for the save to avoid an attack of mad laughter drops by 1d6. When the saving throw DC drops to 0, the creature recovers from the disease. A creature that fails three of these saving throws gains a randomly determined form of indefinite madness, as described in the Dungeon Master's Guide.

### Treatment
- Lesser Restoration or similar magic cures the disease immediately
- Calm Emotions can suppress the laughter temporarily
- Quarantine is recommended to prevent spread

metadata_json.cure: Successive long rest saves (DC reduces by 1d6 per success) or Lesser Restoration

title: Sewer Plague
summary: A disease contracted from filthy conditions, causing exhaustion and preventing recovery during rests.
content:
## Sewer Plague

**Contraction:** Contact with sewage, filthy water, or creatures that dwell in such conditions (giant rats, otyughs)
**Incubation:** 1d4 days

Sewer plague is a generic term for a broad category of illnesses that incubate in sewers, refuse heaps, and stagnant swamps, and which are sometimes transmitted by creatures that dwell in those areas, such as rats and otyughs.

### Effects
When the incubation period ends, the creature begins to suffer. The infected creature gains one level of exhaustion, regains only half the normal number of hit points from spending Hit Dice, and regains no hit points from finishing a long rest.

### Symptoms
- Fatigue and weakness (exhaustion)
- Cramps and muscle aches
- Reduced healing capability
- Fever and chills

### Cure
At the end of each long rest, an infected creature must make a DC 11 Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, the character gains one level of exhaustion. On a successful save, the character's exhaustion level decreases by one level. If a successful saving throw reduces the infected creature's level of exhaustion below 1, the creature recovers from the disease.

### Treatment
- Lesser Restoration or similar magic cures the disease immediately
- Herbal remedies can grant advantage on the saving throw (DM discretion)
- Clean water and rest in sanitary conditions are essential
- Six levels of exhaustion result in death

metadata_json.contraction: Contact with sewage or filthy water
metadata_json.cure: Long rest saves reducing exhaustion to 0, or Lesser Restoration
metadata_json.damage: Exhaustion levels, halved Hit Die healing, no long rest healing

title: Sight Rot
summary: A disease that causes the eyes to cloud over and eventually leads to complete blindness.
content:
## Sight Rot

**Contraction:** Drinking contaminated water or exposure to swamp, marsh, or other stagnant water sources
**Incubation:** 1 day

This painful infection causes bleeding from the eyes and eventually blinds the victim.

### Effects
One day after infection, the creature's vision starts to become blurry. The creature takes a -1 penalty to attack rolls and ability checks that rely on sight. At the end of each long rest after the symptoms appear, the penalty worsens by 1. When the penalty reaches -5, the victim is blinded until its sight is restored by magic such as Lesser Restoration or Heal.

### Progression
| Day | Penalty | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | -1 | Slightly blurry vision |
| 2 | -2 | Noticeably impaired vision |
| 3 | -3 | Severely impaired vision |
| 4 | -4 | Nearly blind |
| 5+ | -5 | Completely blind |

### Cure
Sight rot can be cured using a rare flower called Eyebright, which grows in some swamps. Given an hour, a character who has proficiency with an herbalism kit can turn the flower into one dose of ointment. Applied to the eyes before a long rest, one dose of the ointment prevents the disease from worsening after that rest. After three doses, the ointment cures the disease entirely.

### Treatment
- Lesser Restoration or Heal cures the disease and restores sight
- Eyebright ointment (3 doses over 3 days)
- Once fully blinded, only magic can restore sight

metadata_json.cure: 3 doses of Eyebright ointment, or Lesser Restoration/Heal
metadata_json.damage: Progressive -1 penalty to attack rolls and sight-based checks, eventual blindness

title: Filth Fever
summary: A raging fever caused by filthy wounds, leaving the victim weakened and vulnerable.
content:
## Filth Fever

**Contraction:** Wound inflicted by a creature that dwells in filthy conditions, or an untreated wound exposed to sewage or refuse
**Incubation:** 1d4 days

A raging fever sweeps through the creature's body, leaving it weakened and debilitated.

### Effects
The infected creature suffers the following effects:
- The creature has disadvantage on Strength checks, Strength saving throws, and attack rolls that use Strength
- The creature's hit point maximum is reduced by 1d6 at the end of each long rest
- If the hit point maximum drops to 0, the creature dies

### Symptoms
- High fever with intense sweating
- Muscle weakness and trembling
- Infected wounds become inflamed and swollen
- Delirium in advanced stages

### Cure
At the end of each long rest, the infected creature must make a DC 11 Constitution saving throw. On a successful save, the disease ends. Alternatively:
- Lesser Restoration or similar magic cures it immediately
- A successful DC 15 Wisdom (Medicine) check performed during a long rest grants advantage on the saving throw
- Clean bandages and proper wound care can help prevent infection in the first place

### Prevention
- Clean and bandage wounds promptly
- Use a healer's kit or herbalism kit after combat in filthy environments
- Avoid swimming or wading through sewage with open wounds

metadata_json.contraction: Filthy wound or untreated wound exposure
metadata_json.cure: Successful long rest save (DC 11) or Lesser Restoration
metadata_json.damage: Disadvantage on Strength checks/saves/attacks, HP max reduced by 1d6 per long rest

title: Mindfire
summary: A disease that attacks the mind, causing confusion, hallucinations, and mental deterioration.
content:
## Mindfire

**Contraction:** Exposure to psionic creatures, contact with aberrations, or consuming tainted psychic substances
**Incubation:** 1d4 days

Mindfire is a disease that burns through the victim's mind, causing confusion, hallucinations, and eventual mental deterioration.

### Effects
The infected creature suffers the following effects:
- The creature has disadvantage on Intelligence checks, Intelligence saving throws, and attack rolls that use Intelligence
- The creature must succeed on a DC 12 Constitution saving throw whenever it attempts to cast a spell. On a failure, the spell is lost without effect but the spell slot is still expended
- The creature experiences vivid hallucinations and periods of confusion

### Symptoms
- Severe headaches and migraines
- Vivid, disturbing hallucinations
- Difficulty concentrating or remembering things
- Sporadic periods of confusion and disorientation
- Sensitivity to bright light and loud sounds

### Cure
At the end of each long rest, the infected creature must make a DC 12 Constitution saving throw. On a successful save, the disease ends. Alternatively:
- Lesser Restoration or similar magic cures it immediately
- A Calm Emotions spell can suppress symptoms for its duration
- Psionic healing from a skilled psionicist can cure it

### Who Is Vulnerable
- Spellcasters are particularly devastated by this disease
- Creatures with low Constitution are at greater risk
- Those who frequently interact with aberrations may develop partial immunity (DM discretion)

metadata_json.contraction: Psionic creature exposure or tainted psychic substances
metadata_json.cure: Successful long rest save (DC 12) or Lesser Restoration
metadata_json.damage: Disadvantage on Intelligence checks/saves, chance to lose spells

title: Shivering Sickness
summary: A tropical disease spread by insects, causing uncontrollable shivering and debilitating chills.
content:
## Shivering Sickness

**Contraction:** Insect bites in tropical environments (mosquitoes, jungle flies, and similar insects)
**Incubation:** 1d4 days

Shivering sickness is a tropical disease common in jungles, swamps, and other warm, humid environments. It is spread through the bites of infected insects.

### Effects
The infected creature suffers the following effects:
- The creature has disadvantage on Dexterity checks and Dexterity saving throws
- The creature is vulnerable to cold damage
- The creature suffers uncontrollable shivering that imposes disadvantage on Concentration checks
- At the end of each long rest, the creature must make a DC 12 Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, the creature gains one level of exhaustion

### Symptoms
- Uncontrollable shivering and trembling
- Periodic bouts of intense chills despite warm temperature
- Chattering teeth and muscle spasms
- Difficulty performing precise tasks
- Cold sweats alternating with fever

### Cure
Shivering sickness can be cured by:
- Lesser Restoration or similar magic
- A successful DC 14 Wisdom (Medicine) check during a long rest, using an herbalism kit, grants advantage on that rest's saving throw
- Three consecutive successful DC 12 Constitution saving throws at the end of long rests cure the disease
- Warm baths and herbal teas can provide temporary relief

### Prevention
- Insect repellent (herbal or alchemical)
- Protective clothing in tropical regions
- Sleeping under netting
- Druidic magic to ward off insects

metadata_json.cure: Three consecutive successful saves or Lesser Restoration
metadata_json.damage: Disadvantage on Dex checks/saves, cold vulnerability, exhaustion

title: Bluerot
summary: A disease that causes blue-tinged skin lesions and attacks both body and mind.
content:
## Bluerot

**Contraction:** Contact with contaminated water in coastal or aquatic environments, or wounds inflicted by certain undead sea creatures
**Incubation:** 1d4 days

Bluerot is a disease spread by undead sea creatures and found in stagnant coastal waters. It manifests as blue-tinged lesions on the skin that slowly spread and rot the flesh.

### Effects
The infected creature suffers the following effects:
- The creature has disadvantage on Wisdom saving throws and Charisma checks
- The creature develops visible blue lesions on their skin
- The creature is vulnerable to necrotic damage
- At the end of each long rest, the creature must make a DC 12 Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, the creature takes 7 (2d6) necrotic damage and its hit point maximum is reduced by the same amount. The hit point reduction lasts until the disease is cured

### Symptoms
- Blue-tinged skin lesions that spread over time
- Increasing confusion and paranoia
- Sensitivity to sunlight
- The lesions emit a faint, unpleasant odor
- In advanced stages, the skin begins to visibly rot

### Cure
- Lesser Restoration or similar magic cures the disease and restores lost hit point maximum
- A successful DC 15 Wisdom (Medicine) check with an herbalism kit can create a poultice that grants advantage on the next saving throw
- Three consecutive successful DC 12 Constitution saves cure the disease
- The hit point maximum reduction is only restored when the disease is cured

### Lore
- Common in haunted coastal areas and shipwrecks
- Associated with the undead and necromantic energy
- Some sea hags deliberately spread the disease
- Sailors fear bluerot as a sign of a cursed voyage

metadata_json.contraction: Contaminated coastal water or undead sea creature wounds
metadata_json.cure: Three consecutive saves or Lesser Restoration
metadata_json.damage: 2d6 necrotic per failed save, HP max reduction, disadvantage on Wisdom saves and Charisma checks

title: Throat Leeches
summary: Tiny parasites that infest the throat, causing pain, difficulty breathing, and eventual suffocation.
content:
## Throat Leeches

**Contraction:** Drinking contaminated water containing microscopic leech larvae, common in stagnant jungle pools and swamp water
**Incubation:** 1d4 days

Tiny parasitic leeches that attach to the throat and airways. As they grow, they make breathing increasingly difficult and painful.

### Effects
The infected creature suffers the following effects:
- The creature has disadvantage on Constitution saving throws to maintain concentration
- The creature's voice becomes hoarse and raspy (disadvantage on Charisma checks involving speech)
- At the end of each long rest, the creature must make a DC 13 Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, the creature gains one level of exhaustion as the leeches grow and restrict breathing
- When the creature has 3 or more levels of exhaustion from this disease, it begins to suffocate at the start of each combat (holding its breath rules apply)

### Symptoms
- Sore throat and difficulty swallowing
- Hoarse, raspy voice
- Coughing up small amounts of blood
- Visible movement in the throat (advanced stages)
- Increasingly labored breathing

### Cure
- Lesser Restoration or similar magic kills the leeches and cures the disease
- A successful DC 15 Wisdom (Medicine) check can remove the leeches surgically, but the patient takes 1d6 slashing damage during the procedure
- Drinking a strong salt solution (DC 12 Constitution save to keep it down) kills the leeches over 24 hours
- Some herbalists create a potion from specific jungle herbs that dissolves the leeches

### Prevention
- Always boil or purify water before drinking in tropical regions
- Create Water or Purify Food and Drink spells provide safe drinking water
- Straining water through fine cloth can filter out larvae

metadata_json.cure: Surgical removal (DC 15 Medicine), salt solution, or Lesser Restoration
metadata_json.damage: Exhaustion levels, suffocation risk, disadvantage on concentration and speech

title: Lycanthropy
summary: A curse-disease that transforms the victim into a lycanthrope, such as a werewolf, werebear, or wererat.
content:
## Lycanthropy

**Contraction:** Bitten by or wounded by a lycanthrope in hybrid or animal form
**Incubation:** Varies (symptoms begin at the next full moon or within 1d4 days)

Lycanthropy is both a curse and a disease. A humanoid creature can be afflicted with lycanthropy after being wounded by a lycanthrope, or through heredity (born lycanthropes).

### Types of Lycanthropes
| Type | Alignment | Animal Form | CR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Werebear | Neutral good | Bear | 5 |
| Wereboar | Neutral evil | Boar | 4 |
| Wererat | Lawful evil | Rat | 2 |
| Weretiger | Neutral | Tiger | 4 |
| Werewolf | Chaotic evil | Wolf | 3 |

### Effects
- **Transformation:** The character involuntarily transforms during the full moon (or can learn to control the transformation)
- **Alignment Shift:** The character's alignment gradually shifts toward the lycanthrope type's alignment
- **Stat Changes:** In hybrid or animal form, the character gains the lycanthrope's stat block modifiers
- **Damage Immunity:** In hybrid or animal form, immunity to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing from nonmagical attacks not made with silvered weapons
- **Memory Loss:** Characters who transform involuntarily have no memory of their actions while transformed

### Player Character Lycanthropy
A character who becomes a lycanthrope retains their stats but gains:
- Increased Strength (if the lycanthrope form's Strength is higher)
- Immunity to nonmagical, non-silvered weapon damage (in hybrid/animal form)
- Natural weapons (bite and claws)
- The curse of involuntary transformation

### Cure
- **Remove Curse spell:** Cures an afflicted (non-born) lycanthrope
- **Wish spell:** Can cure even a born lycanthrope
- **Wolfsbane:** Consuming wolfsbane within 1 hour of being bitten allows a DC 20 Constitution save to resist the curse (but wolfsbane itself is poisonous: DC 13 Constitution save or take 2d6 poison damage)
- **Born lycanthropes** (those who inherited the condition) can only be cured by a Wish spell

### DM Considerations
- Lycanthropy can dramatically affect gameplay balance
- Consider whether the character embraces or fights the curse
- The full moon transformation creates compelling story opportunities
- An NPC mentor lycanthrope can help a player character learn control

metadata_json.contraction: Bite or wound from a lycanthrope
metadata_json.damage: Alignment shift, involuntary transformation, potential loss of control

title: Mummy Rot
summary: A supernatural curse-disease inflicted by mummies that prevents healing and slowly kills the victim.
content:
## Mummy Rot

**Contraction:** Touch attack from a mummy (Rotting Fist)
**Incubation:** Immediate

Mummy rot is a supernatural disease and curse inflicted by a mummy's Rotting Fist attack. It is one of the most feared diseases due to its ability to prevent all healing.

### Contraction
If a mummy hits with its Rotting Fist attack, the target must succeed on a DC 12 Constitution saving throw or be cursed with mummy rot. The cursed target can't regain hit points, and its hit point maximum decreases by 10 (3d6) for every 24 hours that elapse. If the curse reduces the target's hit point maximum to 0, the target dies, and its body turns to dust.

### Effects
- **No Healing:** The cursed creature cannot regain hit points by any means (magical or otherwise)
- **HP Maximum Reduction:** Hit point maximum decreases by 10 (3d6) every 24 hours
- **Death:** If HP maximum reaches 0, the creature dies and crumbles to dust
- **Dust Death:** A creature killed by mummy rot cannot be raised except by a Wish spell or divine intervention

### Symptoms
- Skin becomes dry and papery
- Wounds refuse to close or heal
- Gradual desiccation of flesh
- The affected area spreads from the point of contact
- In advanced stages, the flesh begins to crumble like ancient parchment

### Cure
- **Remove Curse** removes the curse but does NOT cure the disease component
- **Heal, Greater Restoration, or Wish** cures both the curse and disease
- Both the curse AND disease must be cured for healing to resume
- Remove Curse followed by curing the disease (Lesser Restoration or similar) also works
- Hit point maximum is restored when both components are eliminated

### Important Rules
- Mummy rot is both a curse AND a disease simultaneously
- Both aspects must be addressed for full recovery
- This is one of the few conditions that can make resurrection difficult
- Creatures reduced to dust by mummy rot require True Resurrection or Wish

metadata_json.cure: Remove Curse + disease cure, or Heal/Greater Restoration/Wish
metadata_json.damage: HP max reduced by 3d6 per 24 hours, no healing possible, death at 0 HP max
